---
title: "On the trails: running and hiking, straight from Strava"
subtitle: "Where I run and hike, how far, and how much climbing, kept current by the Strava API"
description: >
  Trail running and hiking stats, distance, elevation, the long days out on the
  mountain, pulled from the Strava API and refreshed automatically, with photos
  from the trail.
date: 2026-06-26
categories: [running, trail, data, south africa, api]
image: strava-routes.png    # route heatmap, produced on render
image-alt: "Heatmap of my running routes"
freeze: false               # always re-run: the data file updates out of band
draft: true                 # <- remove to publish
---

```{python}
#| label: load
#| echo: false
import json
data = json.load(open("strava_runs.json", encoding="utf-8"))
s = data["stats"]
gen = data["generated_at"][:10]
```

Living under Table Mountain ruins you for flat running. Most weeks end with a few
hours somewhere on the trails above Cape Town, running when the legs are fresh,
hiking when they're not, and Strava quietly records all of it. The numbers below
come from the Strava API and refresh on their own, so this is always the current
picture, not a snapshot I forgot to update.

```{python}
#| label: narrative
#| echo: false
from datetime import date
from IPython.display import Markdown

def sp(n):                       # thousands separated by spaces, like "41 662"
    return f"{int(round(n)):,}".replace(",", " ")

f = date.fromisoformat(s["first_date"])
t = date.fromisoformat(data["generated_at"][:10])
months_total = (t.year - f.year) * 12 + (t.month - f.month) - (1 if t.day < f.day else 0)
yy, mm = divmod(max(months_total, 0), 12)
span_days = max((t - f).days, 1)
since = (f"{yy} years and {mm} months" if yy else f"{mm} months")

secs = s["total_moving_seconds"]
mo, rem = divmod(secs, 30 * 86400); dd, rem = divmod(rem, 86400)
hh, rem = divmod(rem, 3600); mn = rem // 60
mt = " ".join(p for p in [f"{mo}mo" if mo else "", f"{dd}d" if dd else "",
                          f"{hh}h" if hh else "", f"{mn}m" if mn else ""] if p)

km = s["total_distance_km"]
world = km / 40_075                       # Earth's circumference (km)
moon = km / 384_400 * 100                 # mean Earth–Moon distance (km)
everest = s["total_elevation_m"] / 8_849  # Everest height (m)
per_day = km / span_days
pizza = s["total_calories"] / 285         # ~285 kcal per slice

story = (
    f"> Since I first logged an activity on Strava **{since}** ago (on "
    f"**{f:%d-%m-%Y}**), I've been out on **{s['active_days']} days** of training "
    f"and adventure.\n>\n"
    f"> In that time I've covered **{sp(km)} km** — that's **{world:.2f} trips "
    f"around the world** 🌍, or **{moon:.1f}% of the way to the Moon** 🌕 — and "
    f"climbed **{sp(s['total_elevation_m'])} m**, which is **{everest:.1f}× Mount "
    f"Everest** 🏔️. All up that's **{mt}** of moving time 🎉.\n>\n"
    f"> That averages **{sp(per_day)} km a day**, **{sp(per_day*7)} km a week**, "
    f"and **{sp(per_day*30.44)} km a month** 🐣 — powered by an estimated "
    f"**{sp(s['total_calories'])} calories**, or roughly **{sp(pizza)} slices of "
    f"pizza** 🍕."
)
Markdown(story)
```

```{python}
#| label: stat-cards
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML
def card(value, label):
    return (f'<div style="flex:1;min-width:120px;border:1px solid #e2e2e2;'
            f'border-radius:10px;padding:.9rem 1rem;text-align:center">'
            f'<div style="font-size:1.6rem;font-weight:700;color:#0b5e54">{value}</div>'
            f'<div style="font-size:.8rem;color:#666">{label}</div></div>')
HTML('<div style="display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;margin:1rem 0">' + "".join([
    card(f'{s["trail_runs"]}', "trail runs"),
    card(f'{s.get("hikes", 0)}', "hikes"),
    card(f'{s["total_distance_km"]:,} km', "total distance"),
    card(f'{s["total_elevation_m"]:,} m', "elevation gained"),
    card(f'{s["total_moving_hours"]} h', "moving time"),
]) + '</div>')
```

```{python}
#| label: highlights
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
lr, bc = s["longest_run"], s["biggest_climb"]
print(f"The longest single outing so far was **{lr['distance_km']} km** "
      f"(*{lr['name']}*), and the biggest climb was **{bc['elev_gain_m']} m** of ascent.")
```

## From the trail

```{=html}
<!-- Drop a few favourite trail photos into this post's folder (e.g. images/) and
     list them here. They're your own files, so they render reliably offline. -->
```

::: {layout-ncol=3}
![Pipe Track, early light](images/photo-1.jpg){fig-alt="Replace with your photo"}

![Above Hout Bay](images/photo-2.jpg){fig-alt="Replace with your photo"}

![Summit weather](images/photo-3.jpg){fig-alt="Replace with your photo"}
:::

## The shape of a running year

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly
#| fig-cap: "Distance per month (km). Trail volume tracks the seasons — and how busy work is."
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
m = s["by_month"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
ax.bar(list(m), [round(v) for v in m.values()], color="#0b5e54")
ax.set_ylabel("km"); plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig("strava-monthly.png", dpi=130)
```

Trail runs and hikes aren't just longer, they go *up*. Plotting elevation gain
against distance separates the mountain days from the easy road recovery runs:

```{python}
#| label: fig-elev
#| fig-cap: "Elevation gain vs distance. Trail runs and hikes climb far more per kilometre than road runs."
#| echo: false
kinds = {"trail": ("#0b5e54", "trail run", {}),
         "hike":  ("#FC4C02", "hike", {}),
         "run":   ("#888",    "road run", {"facecolors": "none", "edgecolors": "#888"})}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
for k, (color, label, extra) in kinds.items():
    g = [r for r in s["recent"] if r.get("kind", "trail" if r["is_trail"] else "run") == k]
    if not g:
        continue
    ax.scatter([r["distance_km"] for r in g], [r["elev_gain_m"] for r in g],
               s=36, label=label, **({"color": color} if not extra else extra))
ax.set_xlabel("distance (km)"); ax.set_ylabel("elevation gain (m)"); ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
```

## Where I run

Overlaying every recorded track makes a personal heatmap: the lines pile up on
the routes I run most, and the shape of the mountain falls out of the GPS alone.

```{python}
#| label: fig-routes
#| fig-cap: "Every recorded track, overlaid. Start/end points are cropped for privacy."
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
from math import cos, radians
tracks = s["tracks"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
lat_all, lng_all = [], []
for t in tracks:
    lat = [p[0] for p in t]; lng = [p[1] for p in t]
    ax.plot(lng, lat, color="#0b5e54", alpha=0.35, lw=1.2)
    lat_all += lat; lng_all += lng
ax.set_aspect(1 / cos(radians(np.mean(lat_all))))
ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
for sp in ax.spines.values():
    sp.set_visible(False)
fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig("strava-routes.png", dpi=130)
```

Binning the GPS points into a 2-D histogram turns those overlapping lines into a
true density heatmap, the brightest cells are the trails I keep coming back to:

```{python}
#| label: fig-density
#| fig-cap: "GPS-point density (log scale). The hot spots are my most-run trails."
#| echo: false
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
lng = np.array([p[1] for t in tracks for p in t])
lat = np.array([p[0] for t in tracks for p in t])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
h = ax.hist2d(lng, lat, bins=110, cmap="inferno",
              norm=mcolors.LogNorm(), cmin=1)
ax.set_aspect(1 / cos(radians(lat.mean())))
ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
fig.colorbar(h[3], ax=ax, shrink=0.6, label="GPS points")
fig.tight_layout()
```

## When I run

A weekday-by-hour heatmap shows the rhythm: early-morning weekend trail runs, the
odd weekday evening when work allows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-when
#| fig-cap: "Runs by day of week and hour of day."
#| echo: false
grid = np.array(s["time_grid"])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 2.8))
im = ax.imshow(grid, aspect="auto", cmap="Greens")
ax.set_yticks(range(7)); ax.set_yticklabels(["Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"])
ax.set_xticks(range(0, 24, 2)); ax.set_xticklabels([f"{h:02d}" for h in range(0, 24, 2)])
ax.set_xlabel("hour of day")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.8, label="runs")
fig.tight_layout()
```

## What I run in

Joining each activity to the weather at that exact hour (via the free
[Open-Meteo](https://open-meteo.com) archive) shows the conditions I actually
head out in, and whether the heat slows me down.

```{python}
#| label: weather-panel
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML, display
w = s.get("weather", {})
if w.get("available"):
    counts = w["counts"]; mx = max(counts.values())
    LABEL = {"clear": "☀️ clear", "cloudy": "⛅ cloudy", "rain": "🌧️ rain",
             "fog": "🌫️ fog", "storm": "⛈️ storm", "snow": "❄️ snow",
             "other": "· other", "unknown": "· n/a"}
    bars = "".join(
        f'<div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:.5rem;margin:.25rem 0">'
        f'<span style="width:90px;font-size:.85rem">{LABEL.get(k, k)}</span>'
        f'<span style="background:#0b5e54;height:14px;border-radius:3px;'
        f'display:inline-block;width:{round(240 * n / mx)}px"></span>'
        f'<span style="font-size:.8rem;color:#666">{n}</span></div>'
        for k, n in counts.items())
    display(HTML(
        f'<p>Median temperature on the move: <strong>{w["median_temp"]}°C</strong>, '
        f'and about <strong>{w["rain_pct"]}%</strong> of outings caught some rain.</p>'
        + bars))
else:
    display(HTML("<p><em>Weather data unavailable for this snapshot.</em></p>"))
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-pace-temp
#| fig-cap: "Pace vs temperature, one point per outing. Dashed line is a trend fitted to trail runs."
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
pts = w.get("points", [])
if pts:
    KCOL = {"trail": "#0b5e54", "hike": "#FC4C02", "run": "#888"}
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.4))
    for kind in ("trail", "hike", "run"):
        g = [p for p in pts if p["kind"] == kind]
        if g:
            ax.scatter([p["temp"] for p in g], [p["pace"] for p in g], s=24,
                       color=KCOL[kind], label=kind, alpha=0.8, edgecolors="none")
    tr = [p for p in pts if p["kind"] == "trail"]
    if len(tr) > 2:
        T = np.array([p["temp"] for p in tr]); P = np.array([p["pace"] for p in tr])
        b, a0 = np.polyfit(T, P, 1)
        xs = np.linspace(T.min(), T.max(), 50)
        ax.plot(xs, a0 + b * xs, color="#333", lw=1, ls="--")
    ax.set_xlabel("temperature (°C)"); ax.set_ylabel("pace (min/km)"); ax.legend()
    fig.tight_layout()
```

## A few more numbers

```{python}
#| label: fig-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative distance — every kilometre, stacked up over time."
#| echo: false
months = list(s["by_month"])
cum = np.cumsum([s["by_month"][m] for m in months])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 2.8))
ax.plot(months, cum, color="#0b5e54", marker="o", ms=3)
ax.set_ylabel("cumulative km"); plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
fig.tight_layout()
```

### Recent runs

```{python}
#| label: tbl-recent
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import Markdown
rows = ["| Date | Run | km | ↑ m | min/km |", "|---|---|--:|--:|--:|"]
for r in s["recent"][:10]:
    link = f'[{r["name"]}](https://www.strava.com/activities/{r["id"]})'
    kind = r.get("kind", "trail" if r["is_trail"] else "run")
    tag = {"trail": " 🏔️", "hike": " 🥾", "run": ""}.get(kind, "")
    rows.append(f'| {r["date"]} | {link}{tag} | {r["distance_km"]:.1f} '
                f'| {r["elev_gain_m"]} | {r["pace_min_km"]} |')
Markdown("\n".join(rows))
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## How this stays current
A scheduled GitHub Action runs `scripts/fetch_strava.py`, which refreshes a
short-lived Strava access token from a stored refresh token, pulls my activities,
writes `strava_runs.json`, commits it, and re-renders the site. Only my own data
is used (Strava "single player mode"), credentials live in encrypted Action
secrets, and the page links back to each activity on Strava.
:::

```{python}
#| label: attribution
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML
# Strava brand guidelines require visible attribution. Replace the text badge
# below with the official "Powered by Strava" logo (white or orange) from
# https://developers.strava.com/guidelines/ — drop it in as images/api_logo_pwrdBy_strava_horiz_light.svg
HTML(
    '<div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:.6rem;margin-top:2rem;'
    'font-size:.85rem;color:#666">'
    f'<span>Last refreshed {gen}</span><span>·</span>'
    '<span style="background:#FC4C02;color:#fff;font-weight:700;border-radius:4px;'
    'padding:2px 8px;letter-spacing:.02em">Powered by Strava</span>'
    '</div>'
)
```
